summary: Reporter requests that the Dispose method in SKGLView for Blazor be made virtual so subclasses can properly override disposal and clean up their own resources.

stepsToReproduce: Create a class that inherits from SKGLView in Blazor
Attempt to override Dispose to add custom cleanup logic
Observe that Dispose is not virtual and cannot be overridden

analysis.summary: SKGLView.razor.cs implements IDisposable with a non-virtual public Dispose() method at line 190. This prevents subclasses from properly overriding disposal. The same pattern exists in SKCanvasView.razor.cs (line 171). Neither class implements the standard .NET Dispose(bool) pattern. Additionally, SKGLView.Dispose() only disposes dpiWatcher, sizeWatcher, and interop — it does not release native Skia resources (context, glInterface, renderTarget, surface) which could be a separate resource leak concern.
analysis.rationale: This is an enhancement request, not a bug — the existing Dispose works correctly for the base class. The reporter wants extensibility for subclass scenarios. The [FEATURE] tag and the specific ask for 'virtual' confirm this is a design improvement request, not broken behavior.

keySignals.text: SKGLView in blazor implementation has a non-virtual Dispose method, which creates problems when subclassing it.
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter is subclassing SKGLView and cannot properly extend disposal logic.

keySignals.text: Could we have a virtual Dispose method instead?
keySignals.interpretation: Clear, specific ask — make Dispose virtual or implement Dispose(bool) pattern.

codeInvestigation.finding: Dispose() is public non-virtual, implements IDisposable directly. Only disposes dpiWatcher, sizeWatcher, and interop. Does not use the Dispose(bool) pattern.
Same non-virtual Dispose() pattern. Also implements IDisposable directly without Dispose(bool). Calls FreeBitmap() in addition to base cleanup.
Class declaration is 'public partial class SKGLView : IDisposable' — not sealed, so subclassing is expected to work, but Dispose cannot be overridden.

resolution.hypothesis: The Blazor views implement IDisposable directly without the standard Dispose(bool) pattern, preventing subclass cleanup.

proposals.title: Implement standard Dispose(bool) pattern
proposals.description: Refactor SKGLView (and SKCanvasView) to use the standard .NET Dispose pattern with a protected virtual Dispose(bool disposing) method. This is the recommended .NET approach for extensible disposal and would also be the right place to add cleanup of native resources (context, glInterface, renderTarget, surface).

proposals.title: Use composition instead of inheritance
proposals.description: As a workaround, wrap SKGLView inside a custom component using composition rather than subclassing. Handle additional cleanup in the wrapper component's own IDisposable.Dispose() implementation, calling the inner SKGLView's Dispose() from the wrapper.

resolution.recommendedReason: The standard .NET Dispose pattern is the idiomatic solution. It allows subclasses to override Dispose(bool) while maintaining correct disposal semantics. This also creates an opportunity to fix the missing native resource cleanup in SKGLView.Dispose().

actionability.reason: Valid enhancement request. The fix is straightforward (implement Dispose(bool) pattern) and would improve extensibility of Blazor views.

actions.description: Apply enhancement and Blazor area labels

actions.comment:
Thanks for the report. This is a valid request — the Blazor views implement `IDisposable` directly without the standard `Dispose(bool)` pattern, which prevents subclasses from extending disposal.

As a workaround, you can use composition instead of inheritance: wrap `SKGLView` inside your own Blazor component and handle additional cleanup in the wrapper's `Dispose()` method.

The proper fix would be implementing the standard .NET Dispose pattern with a `protected virtual void Dispose(bool disposing)` method. This applies to both `SKGLView` and `SKCanvasView` in the Blazor package.
